**Polish people**, or **Poles**,[[a]](#cite_note-41) are a [West Slavic](/wiki/West_Slav "West Slav") [ethnic group](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") and [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation")[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) who share a common [history](/wiki/History_of_Poland "History of Poland"), [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Poland "Culture of Poland"), the [Polish language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") and are identified with the country of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"). The [preamble](/wiki/Preamble "Preamble") to the [Constitution of the Republic of Poland](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Constitution of the Republic of Poland") defines the Polish nation as comprising all the [citizens](/wiki/Citizenship "Citizenship") of Poland, regardless of heritage or ethnicity. The majority of Poles adhere to [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism").[[40]](#cite_note-Stat-40)

The population of self-declared Poles in Poland is estimated at 37,394,000 out of an overall population of 38,512,000 (based on the 2011 census),[[44]](#cite_note-stat-45) of whom 36,522,000 declared Polish alone.[[45]](#cite_note-gudaszewski-46)[[46]](#cite_note-NSP_2011-47)[[4]](#cite_note-Ludność-4) A wide-ranging Polish diaspora (the *[Polonia](/wiki/Polish_diaspora "Polish diaspora")*) exists throughout [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia"), the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), and [Australasia](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia"). Today, the largest urban concentrations of Poles are within the [Warsaw metropolitan area](/wiki/Warsaw_metropolitan_area "Warsaw metropolitan area") and the [Katowice urban area](/wiki/Katowice_urban_area "Katowice urban area").

Ethnic Poles are considered to be the descendants of the ancient West Slavic [Lechites](/wiki/Lechites "Lechites") and other tribes that inhabited the Polish territories during the [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity") period. Poland's [recorded history](/wiki/Recorded_history "Recorded history") dates back over a thousand years to c. 930–960 AD, when the [Western Polans](/wiki/Polans_(western) "Polans (western)") – an influential tribe in the [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland") region – united various Lechitic clans under what became the [Piast dynasty](/wiki/Piast_dynasty "Piast dynasty"),[[47]](#cite_note-48) thus creating the first Polish state. The subsequent [Christianization of Poland](/wiki/Christianization_of_Poland "Christianization of Poland") by the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), in 966 CE, marked Poland's advent to the community of [Western Christendom](/wiki/Western_Christendom "Western Christendom"). However, throughout its existence, the Polish state followed a tolerant policy towards minorities resulting in numerous ethnic and religious identities of the Poles, such as [Polish Jews](/wiki/Polish_Jews "Polish Jews").

## Exonyms

See also: [Name of Poland](/wiki/Name_of_Poland "Name of Poland")

The Polish [endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym") *Polacy* is derived from the [Western Polans](/wiki/Polans_(western) "Polans (western)"), a [Lechitic](/wiki/Lechites "Lechites") tribe which inhabited lands around the [River Warta](/wiki/Warta "Warta") in [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland") region from the mid-6th century onward.[[48]](#cite_note-:0-49) The tribe's name stems from the [Proto-Indo European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") *\*pleh₂-*, which means flat or flatland and corresponds to the [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography") of a region that the Western Polans initially settled.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51) The prefix *pol-* is used in most world languages when referring to [Poles](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Pole#English "wiktionary:Pole") (Spanish *polaco*, Italian *polacche*, French *polonais*, German *Pole*).

Among other foreign [exonyms](/wiki/Exonym "Exonym") for the Polish people are [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language") *Lenkai*; [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") *Lengyelek*; [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language") *Leh*; [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"): Լեհաստան *Lehastan*; and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): لهستان (*Lahestān*). These stem from [Lechia](/wiki/Lechia "Lechia"), the ancient name for Poland, or from the tribal [Lendians](/wiki/Lendians "Lendians"). Their names are equally derived from the [Old Polish](/wiki/Old_Polish_language "Old Polish language") term *lęda*, meaning plain or field.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

## Ethnogenesis

Main article: [History of Poland](/wiki/History_of_Poland "History of Poland")

See also: [Prehistoric Poland](/wiki/Prehistoric_Poland "Prehistoric Poland"), [Early Slavs](/wiki/Early_Slavs "Early Slavs"), [Western Slavs](/wiki/Western_Slavs "Western Slavs"), and [Lechites](/wiki/Lechites "Lechites")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Gesta_Hamburgensis_trans_oddara_st_polanos.jpg/330px-Gesta_Hamburgensis_trans_oddara_st_polanos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gesta_Hamburgensis_trans_oddara_st_polanos.jpg)

Fragment of *[Gesta Hammaburgensis ecclesiae pontificum](/wiki/Gesta_Hammaburgensis_ecclesiae_pontificum "Gesta Hammaburgensis ecclesiae pontificum")* (1073) by [Adam of Bremen](/wiki/Adam_of_Bremen "Adam of Bremen"), containing the name "[Polans](/wiki/Polans_(western) "Polans (western)")": "*trans [Oddaram](/wiki/Oder "Oder") sunt Polanos*"

The Polish people are descended from a blend of various ancient [ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_groups "Ethnic groups") that inhabited the territory of modern-day Poland before and during [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity").[[52]](#cite_note-Heather-53)[[53]](#cite_note-Łukowski_&_Zawadzki-54) The area was settled by numerous tribes and cultures, including [Baltic](/wiki/Balts "Balts"), [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts"), [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_tribes "Germanic tribes"), [Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs"), [Thracian](/wiki/Thracians "Thracians"), and possibly remnants of earlier [Proto-Indo-Europeans](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans") and non-Indo-European peoples.[[52]](#cite_note-Heather-53) Archaeological evidence from the [Lusatian culture](/wiki/Lusatian_culture "Lusatian culture") (c. 1300–500 BCE), as well as the successive [Pomeranian](/wiki/Pomeranian_culture "Pomeranian culture"), [Przeworsk](/wiki/Przeworsk_culture "Przeworsk culture") and [Wielbark](/wiki/Wielbark_culture "Wielbark culture") cultures, points to a diverse demographic landscape in prehistoric Poland.[[52]](#cite_note-Heather-53) These cultures were associated with different ethnic groups, such as the Celts (notably in southern Poland), Germanic tribes like the [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals") and [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths"), and the Balts in the northeast.[[52]](#cite_note-Heather-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Gimbutas-55)

During the [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period"), the region was becoming increasingly settled by the [early Slavs](/wiki/Early_Slavs "Early Slavs") (c. 500–700 AD).[[55]](#cite_note-kob530-56) The Slavic settlers organised into [tribal units](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") and assimilated the remnants of earlier populations, thus contributing to the [West Slavic](/wiki/West_Slavs "West Slavs") ethnogenesis and identity of the numerous [Polish tribes](/wiki/Polish_tribes "Polish tribes") and [Lechites](/wiki/Lechites "Lechites").[[56]](#cite_note-Curta1-57)[[57]](#cite_note-Klemensiewicz-58) The names of many tribes are found on the list compiled by the anonymous [Bavarian Geographer](/wiki/Bavarian_Geographer "Bavarian Geographer") in the 9th century.[[58]](#cite_note-playground1-59) In the 9th and 10th centuries the tribes gave rise to developed regions along the upper [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula") (the [Vistulans](/wiki/Vistulans "Vistulans")),[[58]](#cite_note-playground1-59) the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") coast and in [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland"). The ultimate tribal undertaking (10th century) resulted in a lasting [political structure](/wiki/Political_system "Political system") and the creation of a Polish [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)").[[59]](#cite_note-Curta2-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61)

## Language

Main article: [Polish language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w.PNG/330px-Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w.PNG)](/wiki/File:Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w.PNG)

*[Book of Henryków](/wiki/Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w "Book of Henryków")*. Highlighted in red is the earliest known sentence written in the [Old Polish language](/wiki/Old_Polish_language "Old Polish language")

[Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") is the native language of most Poles. It is a [West Slavic language](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages") of the [Lechitic group](/wiki/Lechitic_languages "Lechitic languages") and the sole official language in the Republic of Poland. Its written form uses the [Polish alphabet](/wiki/Polish_alphabet "Polish alphabet"), which is the basic [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") with the addition of six [diacritic marks](/wiki/Diacritic_marks "Diacritic marks"), totalling 32 letters. Bearing relation to [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language") and [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"), it has been profoundly influenced by [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") and other languages over the course of history.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) Poland is linguistically homogeneous – nearly 97% of Poland's citizens declare Polish as their mother tongue.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

Polish-speakers use the language in a uniform manner throughout most of Poland, though numerous [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") and a [vernacular language](/wiki/Vernacular_language "Vernacular language") in certain regions coexist alongside [standard](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") Polish. The most common [lects](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)") in Poland are [Silesian](/wiki/Silesian_language "Silesian language"), spoken in [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia"), and [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language"), widely spoken in historic Eastern [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania") ([Pomerelia](/wiki/Pomerelia "Pomerelia")), today in the northwestern part of Poland.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Kashubian possesses its own status as a separate language.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67) The [Goral people](/wiki/Gorals "Gorals") in the mountainous south use their own nonstandard dialect, accenting and different [intonation](/wiki/Intonation_(linguistics) "Intonation (linguistics)").

The geographical distribution of the [Polish language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") was greatly affected by the border changes and population transfers that followed the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") – forced [expulsions](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_from_Poland_during_and_after_World_War_II "Flight and expulsion of Germans from Poland during and after World War II") and resettlement during that period contributed to the country's current linguistic homogeneity.[[67]](#cite_note-Janowska1-68)

## History synopsis

Main article: [History of Poland](/wiki/History_of_Poland "History of Poland")

### Protohistoric

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Kaliska_II_hoard%2C_Lusatian_culture%2C_Poland.jpg/250px-Kaliska_II_hoard%2C_Lusatian_culture%2C_Poland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaliska_II_hoard,_Lusatian_culture,_Poland.jpg)

Artefacts showing the [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy") and [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") making of the [Lusatian culture](/wiki/Lusatian_culture "Lusatian culture"), [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania"), 8th century BCE

During the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") period (c. 5500–2300 BCE), farming communities began to spread across the contemporary Polish lands, introducing [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery"), and domesticated animals.[[68]](#cite_note-Fibiger-69) The [Lengyel](/wiki/Lengyel_culture "Lengyel culture"), [Funnelbeaker](/wiki/Funnelbeaker_culture "Funnelbeaker culture"), and [Globular Amphora cultures](/wiki/Globular_Amphora_culture "Globular Amphora culture") were notable for their megalithic tombs, settlements, and ceramics.[[69]](#cite_note-Darvill-70)[[70]](#cite_note-Florek-71)[[71]](#cite_note-Nogaj-72) The [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") (c. 2300–700 BCE) precipitated considerable advancements in craftsmanship with the emergence of the [Unetice culture](/wiki/Unetice_culture "Unetice culture") and later the [Lusatian culture](/wiki/Lusatian_culture "Lusatian culture"), the latter of which built the fortified settlement at [Biskupin](/wiki/Biskupin "Biskupin") in the 8th century BCE.[[72]](#cite_note-Fagan-73)[[73]](#cite_note-Oxford_Handbook-74) These communities engaged in bronze [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy"), long-distance trade, and complex burial rites, including [urnfield cremation cemeteries](/wiki/Urnfield_culture "Urnfield culture").[[73]](#cite_note-Oxford_Handbook-74) Among some of the significant archaeological or megalithic sites in Poland are [Bodzia](/wiki/Bodzia_Cemetery "Bodzia Cemetery") (cemetery), [Borkowo](/wiki/Borkowo_megalithic_cemetery "Borkowo megalithic cemetery") (cemetery), [Nowa Cerekwia](/wiki/Nowa_Cerekwia "Nowa Cerekwia") (excavations), [Odry](/wiki/Odry,_Poland "Odry, Poland") (stone circles), [Węsiory](/wiki/W%C4%99siory "Węsiory") (stone circles), [Bronocice](/wiki/Bronocice "Bronocice") ([worlds earliest known depictions of a wheeled vehicle](/wiki/Bronocice_pot "Bronocice pot")) and [Wietrzychowice](/wiki/Wietrzychowice,_Kuyavian-Pomeranian_Voivodeship "Wietrzychowice, Kuyavian-Pomeranian Voivodeship") ([Kuyavian Pyramids](/wiki/Kuyavian_Pyramids "Kuyavian Pyramids") mounds).

### Classical

Poland's history during [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") is primarily reconstructed through [archaeological evidence](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology"), as the region lay outside the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and produced few written records.[[74]](#cite_note-Dzięgielewski-75) In the 1st century BCE, the area was inhabited by Celtic tribes, notably the [Boii](/wiki/Boii "Boii"), who established settlements in [Lower Silesia](/wiki/Lower_Silesia "Lower Silesia").[[74]](#cite_note-Dzięgielewski-75) These groups were part of the [La Tène culture](/wiki/La_T%C3%A8ne_culture "La Tène culture"), recognised for advanced metallurgy, intricate ornamentation, and distinctive burial customs.[[74]](#cite_note-Dzięgielewski-75) Evidence from sites along the [Amber Road](/wiki/Amber_Road "Amber Road"), a major trade route linking the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") to the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), indicates Poland's role as a corridor for goods like [amber](/wiki/Amber "Amber") and [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramics "Ceramics") during this period.[[74]](#cite_note-Dzięgielewski-75)

In the early centuries CE, the [Przeworsk culture](/wiki/Przeworsk_culture "Przeworsk culture") flourished in central and southern Poland, succeeding Celtic presence.[[75]](#cite_note-Olędzki-76) The Przeworsk culture is notable for its [cremation burials](/wiki/Cremation "Cremation"), iron weaponry, and Roman imports.[[75]](#cite_note-Olędzki-76) [Roman coins](/wiki/Roman_coins "Roman coins") and military artifacts discovered in the [Kuyavia](/wiki/Kuyavia "Kuyavia") region suggest contact between local populations and the Roman Empire, possibly through trade or mercenary service.[[75]](#cite_note-Olędzki-76) By the 2nd century CE, the [Wielbark culture](/wiki/Wielbark_culture "Wielbark culture"), linked to Germanic peoples, began to dominate northern and central Poland and gradually replaced the earlier [Oksywie culture](/wiki/Oksywie_culture "Oksywie culture").[[76]](#cite_note-Cieśliński-77) The Wielbark people did not bury weapons in graves, a practice distinct from their Przeworsk neighbours, but their cemeteries reveal long-distance contacts through Roman goods, including [Roman glassware](/wiki/Roman_glassware "Roman glassware").[[77]](#cite_note-Kontny-78) The eventual decline of the Roman Empire in the 4th and 5th centuries coincided with the southward relocation of the Goths, leaving behind a cultural vacuum that was gradually filled by [Slavic migration](/wiki/Slavic_migration "Slavic migration").[[78]](#cite_note-Urbańczyk-79) The incoming tribes built defensive settlements called [gords](/wiki/Gord_(archaeology) "Gord (archaeology)") across much of Poland.[[79]](#cite_note-Łoziński_&_Miłobędzki-80)

### Medieval

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Kr%C3%B3l_W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_%C5%81okietek_nagrobek.jpg/250px-Kr%C3%B3l_W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_%C5%81okietek_nagrobek.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kr%C3%B3l_W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_%C5%81okietek_nagrobek.jpg)

One of the earliest surviving tomb effigies of a Polish king – [Władysław I the Elbow-high](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_I_the_Elbow-high "Władysław I the Elbow-high") – at [Wawel Cathedral](/wiki/Wawel_Cathedral "Wawel Cathedral") in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków")

The [medieval history of Poland](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") began in the 10th century with the rise of the [Piast dynasty](/wiki/Piast_dynasty "Piast dynasty").[[80]](#cite_note-Dabrowski-81) Under [Mieszko I](/wiki/Mieszko_I "Mieszko I"), who accepted [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") in 966 AD, Poland entered the sphere of [Western Latin Christendom](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism").[[81]](#cite_note-Ramet-82) This baptism marked the beginning of statehood and allowed the formation of diplomatic ties with the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and the [Papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy").[[81]](#cite_note-Ramet-82) His son, [Bolesław I the Brave](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_I_the_Brave "Bolesław I the Brave"), expanded the kingdom and was crowned the first [King of Poland](/wiki/King_of_Poland "King of Poland") in 1025, establishing Poland as a regional power.[[82]](#cite_note-Curta_2016-83) However, his successors struggled to maintain control, and the country faced internal unrest, succession disputes, and [pagan uprisings](/wiki/Pagan_reaction_in_Poland "Pagan reaction in Poland") that weakened central authority.[[83]](#cite_note-Labuda-84)

In 1079, [Bolesław II the Bold](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_II_the_Bold "Bolesław II the Bold") entered a conflict with the Catholic Church, culminating in the execution of [Bishop Stanislaus](/wiki/Stanislaus_of_Szczepan%C3%B3w "Stanislaus of Szczepanów"), which led to his downfall and exile.[[84]](#cite_note-Melton-85) After the death of [Bolesław III Wrymouth](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_III_Wrymouth "Bolesław III Wrymouth") in 1138, Poland entered a period of [fragmentation](/wiki/Testament_of_Boles%C5%82aw_III_Wrymouth "Testament of Bolesław III Wrymouth"), as the kingdom was divided among his sons into regional duchies.[[85]](#cite_note-86) This weakened central authority and made the country vulnerable to external threats, including devastating [Mongol invasions](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Poland_(disambiguation) "Mongol invasion of Poland (disambiguation)") in the 13th century.[[86]](#cite_note-Krasuski-87)[[87]](#cite_note-Maroń-88) However, the era also saw the growth of towns under [Magdeburg Law](/wiki/Magdeburg_Law "Magdeburg Law"), the settlement of foreign populations, and the founding of many institutions.[[88]](#cite_note-Dembkowski-89)[[89]](#cite_note-Pac-90) The [Teutonic Order](/wiki/Teutonic_Order "Teutonic Order"), invited to confront pagan [Old Prussians](/wiki/Old_Prussians "Old Prussians") by [Konrad I of Masovia](/wiki/Konrad_I_of_Masovia "Konrad I of Masovia"), established its own state in the northeastern Baltic region, eventually becoming a hostile neighbour.[[90]](#cite_note-Biber-91)

Poland’s reunification began under [Władysław I the Elbow-high](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_I_the_Elbow-high "Władysław I the Elbow-high"),[[91]](#cite_note-Kula-92) who was crowned at [Wawel Cathedral](/wiki/Wawel_Cathedral "Wawel Cathedral") in 1320, and continued under his son, [Casimir III the Great](/wiki/Casimir_III_the_Great "Casimir III the Great"), who strengthened royal authority, modernised the legal system, and promoted education by founding the [first Polish university](/wiki/Jagiellonian_University "Jagiellonian University") in 1364.[[92]](#cite_note-Sokol-93) In 1385, the [Union of Krewo](/wiki/Union_of_Krewo "Union of Krewo") united the Kingdom of Poland ([Jadwiga](/wiki/Jadwiga_of_Poland "Jadwiga of Poland")) and the neighbouring [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania") ([Jogaila](/wiki/Jogaila "Jogaila")) under the [Jagiellonian dynasty](/wiki/Jagiellonian_dynasty "Jagiellonian dynasty"), forming a powerful Christian alliance in East-Central Europe.[[93]](#cite_note-Stone1-94) The [Battle of Grunwald](/wiki/Battle_of_Grunwald "Battle of Grunwald") in 1410 marked a turning point in the struggle against the Teutonic State.[[93]](#cite_note-Stone1-94) By the late Middle Ages, Poland had emerged as a major European kingdom with growing political, cultural, and military influence.[[93]](#cite_note-Stone1-94)

### Early modern

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Han_Konopacki_portrait.jpg/250px-Han_Konopacki_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Han_Konopacki_portrait.jpg)

Portrait of a noble couple ([szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta")) in prayer, [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), c. 1625

Between 1500 and the early 17th century, the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") emerged as one of the most powerful and expansive states in Europe.[[94]](#cite_note-Boggis-95) Established through the [Union of Lublin](/wiki/Union_of_Lublin "Union of Lublin") in 1569, it united the Kingdom of Poland and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania under a single elected [monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") and a shared parliament ([Sejm](/wiki/Sejm_of_the_Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Sejm of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth")).[[94]](#cite_note-Boggis-95) Governed by a unique system of noble democracy ([Golden Liberty](/wiki/Golden_Liberty "Golden Liberty")), the Commonwealth was characterised by a politically active nobility ([szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta")) who wielded considerable political influence.[[94]](#cite_note-Boggis-95) This period is often regarded as Poland’s [Golden Age](/wiki/Polish_Golden_Age "Polish Golden Age"), marked by territorial expansion and [Polonisation](/wiki/Polonisation "Polonisation"), but also by religious tolerance enshrined in the [Warsaw Confederation](/wiki/Warsaw_Confederation "Warsaw Confederation") of 1573, and a flourishing of intellectual and cultural life.[[95]](#cite_note-Łupienko-96)[[96]](#cite_note-Bill_&_Lewis-97) However, the death of [Sigismund II Augustus](/wiki/Sigismund_II_Augustus "Sigismund II Augustus") in 1572 ushered in an era of instability driven by the weaknesses of an [elective monarchy](/wiki/Elective_monarchy_in_Poland "Elective monarchy in Poland").[[97]](#cite_note-Butterwick-Pawlikowski-98) The [Vasa dynasty](/wiki/Vasa_dynasty "Vasa dynasty") ruled from 1587 to 1668, beginning with [Sigismund III](/wiki/Sigismund_III_Vasa "Sigismund III Vasa"), who also claimed the [Swedish](/wiki/Polish-Swedish_union "Polish-Swedish union") throne and moved Poland's capital from [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") to [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") in 1596.[[98]](#cite_note-Roszkowski-99)

The mid-17th century marked the beginning of a prolonged period of decline for the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth.[[99]](#cite_note-Allen_&_Rose-100) A series of destructive conflicts severely weakened the state and destabilised its frontiers, notably [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine")'s struggle for independence from Poland during the [Khmelnytsky Uprising](/wiki/Khmelnytsky_Uprising "Khmelnytsky Uprising") (1648) and the [Swedish Deluge](/wiki/Swedish_Deluge "Swedish Deluge") (1655–1660).[[99]](#cite_note-Allen_&_Rose-100) Simultaneous wars with the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and [Russia](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia") further strained the Commonwealth’s resources and exposed its military and administrative vulnerabilities.[[99]](#cite_note-Allen_&_Rose-100) Internally, governance was crippled by the [liberum veto](/wiki/Liberum_veto "Liberum veto"), a parliamentary mechanism that allowed any deputy to block legislation and dissolve the Sejm, rendering meaningful reform nearly impossible.[[100]](#cite_note-Rundell1-101) Although symbolic military successes occurred, most notably [John III Sobieski](/wiki/John_III_Sobieski "John III Sobieski")’s decisive role in the [Battle of Vienna](/wiki/Battle_of_Vienna "Battle of Vienna") (1683), the victories could not compensate for the growing structural dysfunction.[[101]](#cite_note-Thatcher-102)

In the 18th century, the situation deteriorated further.[[102]](#cite_note-Rundell2-103) The [Saxon](/wiki/Electorate_of_Saxony "Electorate of Saxony") kings from the [House of Wettin](/wiki/House_of_Wettin "House of Wettin"), who ruled Poland in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union_of_Poland_and_Saxony "Personal union of Poland and Saxony"), presided over a period of deepening political stagnation and increasing foreign interference.[[102]](#cite_note-Rundell2-103) Despite reformist efforts by [Stanislaus II Augustus](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_II_August "Stanisław II August"), culminating in the progressive [Constitution of May 3, 1791](/wiki/Constitution_of_May_3,_1791 "Constitution of May 3, 1791") aimed to strengthen central authority and modernise the state, these initiatives were met with hostility from neighbouring powers.[[103]](#cite_note-Rundell3-104) In response, [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"), and [Austria](/wiki/Habsburg_Monarchy "Habsburg Monarchy") orchestrated a series of [territorial partitions](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland") in 1772, 1793, and 1795, thus erasing the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth from the map.[[104]](#cite_note-Bertholet-105) By the close of the 18th century, Poland had ceased to exist as a sovereign state, initiating a long period of interchanging foreign rule that would last until the early 20th century.

### Contemporary

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Westverschiebung.Polens.gif/250px-Westverschiebung.Polens.gif)](/wiki/File:Westverschiebung.Polens.gif)

Border changes of Poland after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") resulted in the [expulsion, movement and repatriation of millions of Polish citizens](/wiki/World_War_II_evacuation_and_expulsion "World War II evacuation and expulsion")

After 123 years of partition, Poland regained independence in 1918 at the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), forming the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") under the leadership of [Józef Piłsudski](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Pi%C5%82sudski "Józef Piłsudski").[[105]](#cite_note-Wandycz-106) The interwar period (1918–1939) was marked by efforts to consolidate borders, build a modern state, and manage deep political divisions.[[106]](#cite_note-Machray-107)[[107]](#cite_note-Porter-Szücs-108) In 1939, Poland was [invaded](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") from the west and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") from the east, triggering [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[108]](#cite_note-Moorhouse-109) The country was occupied, but maintained its sovereignty through the establishment of the [Polish Government-in-Exile](/wiki/Polish_Government-in-Exile "Polish Government-in-Exile"), initially based in [France](/wiki/France "France") and later in [London](/wiki/London "London").[[109]](#cite_note-Tucker-110) This government coordinated resistance efforts at home through the [Home Army](/wiki/Home_Army "Home Army") (*Armia Krajowa*), one of the largest underground movements in German-occupied Europe.[[109]](#cite_note-Tucker-110) Despite this, Poland suffered immense human and material losses, including the deaths of around six million Polish citizens, half of them [Polish Jews](/wiki/Polish_Jews "Polish Jews") who perished in various [Nazi concentration and extermination camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_and_extermination_camps "Nazi concentration and extermination camps") during the [Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[109]](#cite_note-Tucker-110)

After the war, Poland fell within the [Soviet sphere of political influence](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") and became a [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") country, the [Polish People's Republic](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic"), under a one-party regime headed by the [Polish United Workers' Party](/wiki/Polish_United_Workers%27_Party "Polish United Workers' Party").[[110]](#cite_note-Gwiazda-111) The postwar period was marked by centralised planning, [nationalisation](/wiki/Nationalisation "Nationalisation") of industry, and political repression, including [censorship](/wiki/Censorship "Censorship") and the suppression of dissents.[[111]](#cite_note-Źrałka-112) Despite periods of relative stability, widespread dissatisfaction with economic hardship and lack of political freedom persisted.[[111]](#cite_note-Źrałka-112) This discontent culminated in the emergence of the [Solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity_(Polish_trade_union) "Solidarity (Polish trade union)") (Solidarność) movement in the early 1980s, led by [Lech Wałęsa](/wiki/Lech_Wa%C5%82%C4%99sa "Lech Wałęsa"), which began as an independent trade union and evolved into a broader social and political force.[[111]](#cite_note-Źrałka-112) Following the end of [martial law](/wiki/Martial_law_in_Poland "Martial law in Poland") (1981–1983), facing economic crisis and mounting internal pressure, the communist government entered negotiations with opposition leaders, leading to the [Round Table Talks](/wiki/Polish_Round_Table_Talks "Polish Round Table Talks") and the [1989 Polish parliamentary election](/wiki/1989_Polish_parliamentary_election "1989 Polish parliamentary election").[[111]](#cite_note-Źrałka-112)[[112]](#cite_note-Bush-113) These events marked the beginning of a peaceful transition to [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") and to the establishment of the contemporary Third Polish Republic.[[112]](#cite_note-Bush-113)

Since 1989, Poland has undergone significant political, economic, and social transformation. The country transitioned from a centrally [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") to a [market-based system](/wiki/Market-based_economy "Market-based economy") and joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") in 1999 and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") in 2004.[[113]](#cite_note-Jain-114) Poland remains a key player in Central Europe, with a growing economy, strong civil society, and a significant role in supporting regional security.

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Poland](/wiki/Culture_of_Poland "Culture of Poland")

The culture of Poland is closely connected with its intricate 1,000-year [history](/wiki/History_of_Poland "History of Poland"), and forms an important constituent in the [Western civilisation](/wiki/Western_world "Western world").[[114]](#cite_note-115) Strong ties with the Latinate world and the [Roman Catholic faith](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_church "Roman Catholic church") also shaped Poland's cultural identity.[[115]](#cite_note-Rocca_2022-116)[[116]](#cite_note-Graf-117) Various regions in Poland such as [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland"), [Lesser Poland](/wiki/Lesser_Poland "Lesser Poland"), [Kuyavia](/wiki/Kuyavia "Kuyavia"), [Masovia](/wiki/Masovia "Masovia"), [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia"), and [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania") developed their own distinct cultures, cuisines, [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") and dialects. Also, Poland for centuries was a refuge to countless ethnic and religious minorities, who became an important part of Polish society and similarly developed their own unique customs.[[117]](#cite_note-Zarnowski-118)

### Symbols

Main article: [National symbols of Poland](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Poland "National symbols of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/KrakkoFotoThalerTamas20.jpg/250px-KrakkoFotoThalerTamas20.jpg)](/wiki/File:KrakkoFotoThalerTamas20.jpg)

The [Polish White Eagle](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Poland "Coat of arms of Poland") is Poland's enduring [national and cultural symbol](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Poland "National symbols of Poland")

The [Constitution of Poland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Poland "Constitution of Poland") from 1997 defines official state symbols of the Third Polish Republic as: the crowned [white-tailed eagle](/wiki/White-tailed_eagle "White-tailed eagle") (*bielik*, *orzeł biały*) embedded on the [coat of arms of Poland](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Poland "Coat of arms of Poland") (*godło*),[[118]](#cite_note-const-119) the white and red [flag of Poland](/wiki/Flag_of_Poland "Flag of Poland") (*flaga*), and the [national anthem](/wiki/Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost "Poland Is Not Yet Lost").[[118]](#cite_note-const-119) The national colours along with variants of the white eagle often feature on banners, [cockades](/wiki/Cockade "Cockade"), pins, and memorabilia. Among other unofficial and more nature-based symbols is the [white stork](/wiki/White_stork "White stork") (*bocian*), the [European bison](/wiki/European_bison "European bison") (*żubr*), the [red poppy flower](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas") (*mak*), the [oak tree](/wiki/Quercus_robur "Quercus robur") (*dąb*), and the [apple](/wiki/Apple "Apple") (*jabłko*) as the country's national fruit.[[119]](#cite_note-Aniskiewicz-120)[[120]](#cite_note-Ruda-121) [Polonia](/wiki/Polonia_(personification) "Polonia (personification)") has been the [national personification](/wiki/National_personification "National personification") and embodiment of Poland; it represents an allegorical female figure that personifies the Polish nation, much like [Britannia](/wiki/Britannia "Britannia") for [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") or [Marianne](/wiki/Marianne "Marianne") for [France](/wiki/France "France").[[119]](#cite_note-Aniskiewicz-120)

### Names and speech etiquette

Main article: [Polish names](/wiki/Polish_names "Polish names")

In Poland, naming conventions are governed by well-defined linguistic and cultural norms.[[121]](#cite_note-rjp-122) Polish naming laws set by the [Polish Language Council](/wiki/Polish_Language_Council "Polish Language Council") strictly ensure consistency with linguistic rules.[[121]](#cite_note-rjp-122) A full name typically comprises one or two given names followed by a surname. Given names, of various linguistic origins, are often associated with [name days](/wiki/Name_days_in_Poland "Name days in Poland") (*imieniny*) that were once widely celebrated.[[122]](#cite_note-Gliński1-123) [Surnames](/wiki/Surnames "Surnames") are generally inherited and reflect grammatical gender; for instance, the masculine form *Kowalski* corresponds to the feminine *Kowalska*.[[123]](#cite_note-Gliński2-124) Some surnames, such as *Nowak*, remain unchanged regardless of gender. [Plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural") forms are also used when referring to families, such as *Kowalscy*.[[123]](#cite_note-Gliński2-124)

Many surnames derive from occupational titles, geographic locations, or descriptive traits.[[123]](#cite_note-Gliński2-124) Since the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), Polish-sounding surnames ending with the masculine *-ski* [suffix](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") and the corresponding feminine suffix *-ska* were associated with the [nobility](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta").[[124]](#cite_note-SCL-125) Nobles also utilised [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") [naming conventions](/wiki/Roman_naming_conventions "Roman naming conventions"), including [agnomens](/wiki/Agnomen "Agnomen"). In speech etiquette, the [Polish language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") maintains strict [T–V distinction](/wiki/T%E2%80%93V_distinction "T–V distinction") pronouns, honorifics and formalities when addressing individuals in [vocative case](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case") (*Pan* for an adult man, *Pani* for an adult woman, *Panna* for a young unmarried woman); the extent to which affectionate forms or name [diminutives](/wiki/Diminutives "Diminutives") are used can vary.[[125]](#cite_note-Hajduk-126)

### Literature

Main article: [Polish literature](/wiki/Polish_literature "Polish literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Psa%C5%82terz_florianski1.jpg/250px-Psa%C5%82terz_florianski1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Psa%C5%82terz_florianski1.jpg)

[Saint Florian's Psalter](/wiki/Sankt_Florian_Psalter "Sankt Florian Psalter") one of the earliest surviving works in the [Polish language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), held at the [National Library of Poland](/wiki/National_Library_of_Poland "National Library of Poland")

According to a 2020 study, Poland ranks 12th globally on a list of countries which read the most, and approximately 79% of Poles read the news more than once a day, placing it second behind [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden").[[126]](#cite_note-127) As of 2021, six Poles received the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature").[[b]](#cite_note-128) The national epic is *[Pan Tadeusz](/wiki/Pan_Tadeusz "Pan Tadeusz")* (English: Master Thaddeus), written by [Adam Mickiewicz](/wiki/Adam_Mickiewicz "Adam Mickiewicz"). Renowned novelists who gained much recognition abroad include [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad") (wrote in English; *[Heart of Darkness](/wiki/Heart_of_Darkness "Heart of Darkness")*, *[Lord Jim](/wiki/Lord_Jim "Lord Jim")*), [Stanisław Lem](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Lem "Stanisław Lem") (science-fiction; *[Solaris](/wiki/Solaris_(novel) "Solaris (novel)")*) and [Andrzej Sapkowski](/wiki/Andrzej_Sapkowski "Andrzej Sapkowski") (fantasy; *[The Witcher](/wiki/The_Witcher "The Witcher")*).

Earliest examples of [Polish literature](/wiki/Polish_literature "Polish literature") date back to the 10th and 11th centuries, when they were primarily written in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), with religious texts and chronicles like [Gesta principum Polonorum](/wiki/Gesta_principum_Polonorum "Gesta principum Polonorum") by [Gallus Anonymus](/wiki/Gallus_Anonymus "Gallus Anonymus") (early 12th century) providing a foundational account of Poland's early rulers.[[127]](#cite_note-Gesta-129) The [Holy Cross Sermons](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Sermons "Holy Cross Sermons") are the oldest extant prose texts in Polish, dating from the 14th century and preserved only in fragments.[[128]](#cite_note-Wydra-130) [Saint Florian's Psalter](/wiki/Sankt_Florian_Psalter "Sankt Florian Psalter"), a trilingual manuscript in Latin, Polish, and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), is one of the earliest complete translations of the [Psalms](/wiki/Psalm "Psalm"), and the [Bible of Queen Sophia](/wiki/Bible_of_Queen_Sophia "Bible of Queen Sophia") is the [first complete translation of the Bible into Polish](/wiki/Bible_translations_into_Polish "Bible translations into Polish").[[128]](#cite_note-Wydra-130) This period of Latin-dominated writing gradually gave way to the use of the Polish language in literature during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") ([Mikołaj Rey](/wiki/Miko%C5%82aj_Rey "Mikołaj Rey") and [Jan Kochanowski](/wiki/Jan_Kochanowski "Jan Kochanowski")) in the 16th century.[[129]](#cite_note-131)

### Education and sciences

Main article: [Education in Poland](/wiki/Education_in_Poland "Education in Poland")

Personal achievement and education plays an important role in Polish society today. In 2018, the [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") ranked Poland 11th in the world for mathematics, science and reading.[[130]](#cite_note-132) Education has been of prime interest to Poland since the early 12th century, particularly for its [noble classes](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta"). In 1364, King [Casimir the Great](/wiki/Casimir_the_Great "Casimir the Great") founded the [Kraków Academy](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), which would become [Jagiellonian University](/wiki/Jagiellonian_University "Jagiellonian University"), the second-oldest institution of higher learning in Central Europe.[[131]](#cite_note-133)

Poland made important contributions to science, particularly during the Renaissance and [Enlightenment](/wiki/Enlightenment_in_Poland "Enlightenment in Poland"); among the chief figures was [Nicolaus Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus"), who revolutionised [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") with his [heliocentric theory](/wiki/Heliocentric_theory "Heliocentric theory"). During the [partitions](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland") in the 18th and 19th centuries, scientific societies and educational efforts kept knowledge alive.[[132]](#cite_note-Materka-134) The [Warsaw Society of Friends of Learning](/wiki/Warsaw_Society_of_Friends_of_Learning "Warsaw Society of Friends of Learning"), Kraków's [Polish Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Polish_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Polish Academy of Arts and Sciences") and secret teaching networks ([Flying University](/wiki/Flying_University "Flying University")) played crucial roles in preserving intellectual life.[[132]](#cite_note-Materka-134) In the 20th century, Poland produced several [Nobel Prize winners](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates "List of Nobel laureates") in science, including [Marie Skłodowska–Curie](/wiki/Marie_Sk%C5%82odowska%E2%80%93Curie "Marie Skłodowska–Curie"), a pioneer in [radioactivity](/wiki/Radioactivity "Radioactivity"). People of Polish birth or citizenship have also made considerable contributions in the fields of philosophy, psychology, technology and mathematics both in Poland and abroad,[[133]](#cite_note-135) among them [Alfred Tarski](/wiki/Alfred_Tarski "Alfred Tarski"), [Benoit Mandelbrot](/wiki/Benoit_Mandelbrot "Benoit Mandelbrot"), [Bronisław Malinowski](/wiki/Bronis%C5%82aw_Malinowski "Bronisław Malinowski"), [Leonid Hurwicz](/wiki/Leonid_Hurwicz "Leonid Hurwicz"), [Leszek Kołakowski](/wiki/Leszek_Ko%C5%82akowski "Leszek Kołakowski"), [Ralph Modjeski](/wiki/Ralph_Modjeski "Ralph Modjeski"), [Rudolf Weigl](/wiki/Rudolf_Weigl "Rudolf Weigl"), [Solomon Asch](/wiki/Solomon_Asch "Solomon Asch"), [Stefan Banach](/wiki/Stefan_Banach "Stefan Banach"), and [Stanisław Ulam](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Ulam "Stanisław Ulam").

### Music and dance

Main article: [Music of Poland](/wiki/Music_of_Poland "Music of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Chopin_concert.jpg/330px-Chopin_concert.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chopin_concert.jpg)

[Frédéric Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin") plays in the salon for the [Radziwiłł family](/wiki/Radziwi%C5%82%C5%82_family "Radziwiłł family"), 1829. Painting by [Henryk Siemiradzki](/wiki/Henryk_Siemiradzki "Henryk Siemiradzki") from 1887

Traditional [Polish music](/wiki/Polish_music "Polish music") is characterised by distinctive regional styles and features folk instruments such as the [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion"), and [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet").[[134]](#cite_note-Gameiro-136) Particularly notable is the highland [bagpipe](/wiki/Bagpipe "Bagpipe") and fiddle music from the [Tatra Mountains](/wiki/Tatra_Mountains "Tatra Mountains"), recognised for its dynamic rhythms and expressive melodies. Poland has also made significant contributions to the [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") canon, most prominently through the works of Polish pianist and composer [Frédéric Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin"), whose compositions remain central to the [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_music "Romantic music") repertoire.

The [Polish folk dances](/wiki/Polish_folk_dances "Polish folk dances"), including the [polonaise](/wiki/Polonaise "Polonaise"), [mazurka](/wiki/Mazurka "Mazurka"), [krakowiak](/wiki/Krakowiak "Krakowiak") (cracovienne), [oberek](/wiki/Oberek "Oberek"), and [kujawiak](/wiki/Kujawiak "Kujawiak"), feature diverse rhythmic structures, tempos and choreographic patterns.[[134]](#cite_note-Gameiro-136) Moreover, the [polka](/wiki/Polka "Polka") resonated with Polish dance traditions and was incorporated into local repertoires.[[135]](#cite_note-Silverman-137) The dance tunes were popularised by Chopin in Europe and by the [Polish-American](/wiki/Polish-American "Polish-American") community in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America").[[136]](#cite_note-138)[[135]](#cite_note-Silverman-137)

[Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") songs and religious hymns such as [Gaude Mater Polonia](/wiki/Gaude_Mater_Polonia "Gaude Mater Polonia") and [Bogurodzica](/wiki/Bogurodzica "Bogurodzica") were once chanted in places of worship and during festivities, but the tradition has faded.[[137]](#cite_note-Sturman-139) [Sung poetry](/wiki/Sung_poetry "Sung poetry"), [disco polo](/wiki/Disco_polo "Disco polo") and [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") remain important in Poland’s musical identity, the latter supported by a strong tradition dating back to the mid-20th century.[[138]](#cite_note-Wilson1-140) In modern times, [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") has emerged as one of the most influential genres among younger audiences, often characterised by its strong connection to urban culture.[[138]](#cite_note-Wilson1-140)

### Art

Main articles: [Art of Poland](/wiki/Art_of_Poland "Art of Poland") and [Polish folk costumes](/wiki/Polish_folk_costumes "Polish folk costumes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Polish_youth_during_a_folk_festival_in_Krzczon%C3%B3w.jpg/250px-Polish_youth_during_a_folk_festival_in_Krzczon%C3%B3w.jpg)](/wiki/File:Polish_youth_during_a_folk_festival_in_Krzczon%C3%B3w.jpg)

Two men and a woman in folk costumes from the [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin "Lublin") region, 1938

During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_in_Poland "Renaissance in Poland"), Poland absorbed [Western European](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") artistic influences while developing its own unique expressions.[[139]](#cite_note-Wilson2-141) [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture"), in particular [Brick Gothic](/wiki/Brick_Gothic "Brick Gothic"), as well as religious iconography, and [illuminated manuscripts](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript") flourished in the medieval period, followed by a Renaissance golden age in music and architecture influenced by [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands").[[139]](#cite_note-Wilson2-141)[[140]](#cite_note-Stone2-142) Artists like [Jan Matejko](/wiki/Jan_Matejko "Jan Matejko") in the 19th century brought national history to life with [historical painting](/wiki/Historical_painting "Historical painting"), which played a significant role in fostering Polish identity.[[139]](#cite_note-Wilson2-141) In the 20th and 21st centuries, Polish art reflected the country’s shifting political and cultural landscape; the various styles comprised [Modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism"), [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco"), [Surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"), [Socialist Realism](/wiki/Socialist_Realism "Socialist Realism") and [Abstract art](/wiki/Abstract_art "Abstract art"). In general, Polish art is deeply engaged with questions of history, identity, and resilience.[[141]](#cite_note-Tobey-143)

The use of colourful flower motifs, [woodworking](/wiki/Woodworking "Woodworking"), [papercutting](/wiki/Papercutting "Papercutting"), and [needlework](/wiki/Needlework "Needlework") are important parts of Polish folk art.[[142]](#cite_note-Anderson-144) Traditional [Polish folk costumes](/wiki/Polish_folk_costumes "Polish folk costumes") (*stroje ludowe*) often feature rich embroidery, vivid colours, and decorative elements such as beads, ribbons, and [lace](/wiki/Lace "Lace").[[143]](#cite_note-Sznajder-145) Women’s attire typically includes long skirts, [aprons](/wiki/Apron "Apron"), embroidered blouses, [corsets](/wiki/Corset "Corset") or vests, and headscarves or [wreaths](/wiki/Wreath_(attire) "Wreath (attire)"),[[143]](#cite_note-Sznajder-145) while men’s outfits often feature embroidered shirts, sashes, hats, and [high boots](/wiki/Jackboots "Jackboots").[[144]](#cite_note-PolishFolk-146) Some of the most well-known regional costumes include the [Łowicz](/wiki/%C5%81owicz "Łowicz") garb, the [Goral](/wiki/Goral "Goral") (Highlander) clothing from the [Tatra Mountains](/wiki/Tatra_Mountains "Tatra Mountains"), and the Kraków costume, often considered Poland's national dress.[[144]](#cite_note-PolishFolk-146) [Rogatywka](/wiki/Rogatywka "Rogatywka"), also known as a "konfederatka", is a type of hat which originated in Poland and is worn by the [military](/wiki/Polish_Land_Forces "Polish Land Forces").

### Food culture

Main article: [Polish cuisine](/wiki/Polish_cuisine "Polish cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/02_Galizische_Spezialit%C3%A4ten%2C_Rudawka_Rymanowska_2013.JPG/250px-02_Galizische_Spezialit%C3%A4ten%2C_Rudawka_Rymanowska_2013.JPG)](/wiki/File:02_Galizische_Spezialit%C3%A4ten,_Rudawka_Rymanowska_2013.JPG)

[Kielbasa](/wiki/Kielbasa "Kielbasa") sausages; meat and [pork](/wiki/Pork "Pork") consumption per capita in Poland is high

Meals are typically structured around three main parts: breakfast (*śniadanie*), dinner (*obiad*), the largest meal of the day, and [supper](/wiki/Supper "Supper") (*kolacja*), though eating [second breakfast](/wiki/Second_breakfast "Second breakfast") (*drugie śniadanie*) or evening snacks is characteristic for Poland.[[145]](#cite_note-CulturePL1-147) Popular everyday foods include pork cutlets ([kotlet schabowy](/wiki/Kotlet_schabowy "Kotlet schabowy")), schnitzels, [kielbasa](/wiki/Kielbasa "Kielbasa") sausage, potatoes, [coleslaw](/wiki/Coleslaw "Coleslaw") and salads, soups ([barszcz](/wiki/Barszcz "Barszcz"), [tomato](/wiki/Tomato_soup "Tomato soup") or [meat broth](/wiki/Ros%C3%B3%C5%82 "Rosół")), [pierogi](/wiki/Pierogi "Pierogi") dumplings, and various types of [bread](/wiki/Bread "Bread") ([kaiser rolls](/wiki/Kaiser_roll "Kaiser roll"), [rye bread](/wiki/Rye_bread "Rye bread"), [bagels](/wiki/Bagels "Bagels")). Polish cuisine also reflects strong seasonal and religious influences; during [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), traditional dishes become meatless, often featuring fish like [herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring") or [carp](/wiki/Carp "Carp"), while [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve") ([Wigilia](/wiki/Wigilia "Wigilia")) is celebrated with a [twelve-dish vegetarian meal](/wiki/Twelve-dish_Christmas_Eve_supper "Twelve-dish Christmas Eve supper").[[146]](#cite_note-Allen_&_Lipska-148)

Traditional Polish cuisine is hearty and Poles are one of the more obese nations in Europe – approximately 58% of the adult population was overweight in 2019, above the EU average.[[147]](#cite_note-149) According to data from 2017, [meat consumption per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_meat_consumption "List of countries by meat consumption") in Poland was one of the highest in the world, with [pork](/wiki/Pork "Pork") being the most in demand.[[148]](#cite_note-FAO2017-150) [Vegetarianism](/wiki/Vegetarianism "Vegetarianism") is on the rise, though this is not measured by the [Statistical Office](/wiki/Statistics_Poland "Statistics Poland"). [Alcohol consumption](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_alcohol_consumption_per_capita "List of countries by alcohol consumption per capita") is relatively moderate compared to other European states;[[149]](#cite_note-151) popular alcoholic beverages include Polish-produced [beer](/wiki/Beer "Beer"), [vodka](/wiki/Vodka "Vodka") and [ciders](/wiki/Cider "Cider").

## Religion

Main article: [Religion in Poland](/wiki/Religion_in_Poland "Religion in Poland")

See also: [Roman Catholicism in Poland](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Poland "Roman Catholicism in Poland"), [Polish National Catholic Church](/wiki/Polish_National_Catholic_Church "Polish National Catholic Church"), [Polish Orthodox Church](/wiki/Polish_Orthodox_Church "Polish Orthodox Church"), [Polish Lutheran Church](/wiki/Polish_Lutheran_Church "Polish Lutheran Church"), [Pentecostal Church in Poland](/wiki/Pentecostal_Church_in_Poland "Pentecostal Church in Poland"), [Baptist Union of Poland](/wiki/Baptist_Union_of_Poland "Baptist Union of Poland"), and [Polish Reformed Church](/wiki/Polish_Reformed_Church "Polish Reformed Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Pope_John_Paul_II_in_Krak%C3%B3w_%E2%80%93_1983.jpg/250px-Pope_John_Paul_II_in_Krak%C3%B3w_%E2%80%93_1983.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pope_John_Paul_II_in_Krak%C3%B3w_%E2%80%93_1983.jpg)

[John Paul II](/wiki/John_Paul_II "John Paul II") was the first Pole to become a [Roman Catholic Pope](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Pope "Roman Catholic Pope")

Poles have traditionally adhered to the Christian faith; an overwhelming majority belongs to the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"),[[150]](#cite_note-catholic-152) with 87.5% of Poles in 2011 identifying as [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic").[[151]](#cite_note-GUS99-153) According to [Poland's Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Constitution of the Republic of Poland"), freedom of religion is ensured to everyone. It also allows for national and ethnic minorities to have the right to establish educational and cultural institutions, institutions designed to protect religious identity, as well as to participate in the resolution of matters connected with their cultural identity.

There are smaller communities primarily comprising [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") (especially [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutherans "Lutherans")), [Orthodox Christians](/wiki/Polish_Orthodox_Church "Polish Orthodox Church") (migrants), [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), those [irreligious](/wiki/Irreligious "Irreligious"), and [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") (mostly from the [Jewish populations in Poland](/wiki/Polish_Jews "Polish Jews") who have lived in Poland prior to World War II)[[152]](#cite_note-154) and [Sunni Muslims](/wiki/Sunni_Muslims "Sunni Muslims") ([Polish Tatars](/wiki/Polish_Tatars "Polish Tatars")). Roman Catholics live all over the country, while Orthodox Christians can be found mostly in the far north-eastern corner, in the area of [Białystok](/wiki/Bia%C5%82ystok "Białystok"), and Protestants in [Cieszyn Silesia](/wiki/Cieszyn_Silesia "Cieszyn Silesia") and [Warmia-Masuria](/wiki/Warmia-Masuria "Warmia-Masuria") regions. A growing Jewish population exists in major cities, especially in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") and [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"). Over two million Jews of Polish origin reside in the United States, Brazil, and Israel.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Wojciech_Gerson%2C_Przyj%C4%99cie_%C5%BByd%C3%B3w.jpg/250px-Wojciech_Gerson%2C_Przyj%C4%99cie_%C5%BByd%C3%B3w.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wojciech_Gerson,_Przyj%C4%99cie_%C5%BByd%C3%B3w.jpg)

[Casimir III the Great](/wiki/Casimir_III_the_Great "Casimir III the Great") welcomes the [Jews](/wiki/Polish_Jews "Polish Jews") to Poland. Painting by [Wojciech Gerson](/wiki/Wojciech_Gerson "Wojciech Gerson")

Religious organisations in the Republic of Poland can register their institution with the [Ministry of Interior and Administration](/wiki/Ministry_of_Interior_and_Administration_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Ministry of Interior and Administration of the Republic of Poland") creating a record of churches and other religious organisations who operate under separate Polish laws. This registration is not necessary; however, it is beneficial when it comes to serving the freedom of religious practice laws.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Slavic Native Faith](/wiki/Slavic_Native_Faith "Slavic Native Faith") (*Rodzimowiercy*) groups, registered with the Polish authorities in 1995, are the [Native Polish Church](/wiki/Native_Polish_Church "Native Polish Church") (*Rodzimy Kościół Polski*), which represents a pagan tradition going back to [Władysław Kołodziej](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Ko%C5%82odziej "Władysław Kołodziej")'s 1921 Holy Circle of Worshippers of [Światowid](/wiki/Svetovid "Svetovid") (*Święte Koło Czcicieli Światowida*), and the Polish Slavic Church (*Polski Kościół Słowiański*). There is also the [Native Faith Association](/wiki/Rodzima_Wiara "Rodzima Wiara") (*Zrzeszenie Rodzimej Wiary*, *ZRW*), founded in 1996.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

Polish people are the fifth-largest national group in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU) after Germans, French, Italians and Spaniards.[[154]](#cite_note-156) Estimates vary depending on source, though available data suggest a total number of up to 60 million people worldwide (with up to 22 million living outside of Poland).[[1]](#cite_note-polmap-1) There are almost 38 million Poles in Poland alone.[[155]](#cite_note-:02-157) There are also strong Polish communities in neighbouring countries, whose territories were once occupied or part of Poland – western [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus"), western [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") and in the [Cieszyn Silesia](/wiki/Cieszyn_Silesia "Cieszyn Silesia") region of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic").[[1]](#cite_note-polmap-1)

The term "[Polonia](/wiki/Polish_diaspora "Polish diaspora")" is usually used in Poland to refer to people of Polish origin who live outside Polish borders. There is a notable Polish [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora") in the [United States](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American"), [Brazil](/wiki/Polish_Brazilian "Polish Brazilian"), and [Canada](/wiki/Polish_Canadians "Polish Canadians"). France has a historic relationship with Poland and has a relatively large Polish-descendant population. Poles have lived in France since the 18th century. In the early 20th century, over a million Polish people settled in [France](/wiki/France "France"), mostly during world wars, among them Polish émigrés fleeing either [Nazi occupation](/wiki/Occupation_of_Poland_(1939%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Poland (1939–1945)") (1939–1945) or [Communism](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic") (1945/1947–1989). There is also a notable Polish diaspora in the United Kingdom and in Germany.[[1]](#cite_note-polmap-1)

In the United States, a significant number of Polish immigrants settled in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") (billed as the world's most Polish city outside of Poland), Milwaukee, Ohio, Detroit, [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), New York City, [Orlando](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida"), [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), and [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"). The highest concentration of [Polish Americans](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans") in a single New England municipality is in [New Britain, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Britain,_Connecticut "New Britain, Connecticut"). In year 1900 the largest Catholic Polish communities in the United States were in Pennsylvania, New York State, Illinois, Wisconsin and Michigan.[[156]](#cite_note-158) The majority of Polish Canadians have arrived in Canada since World War II. The number of Polish immigrants increased between 1945 and 1970, and again after the [end of Communism in Poland in 1989](/wiki/End_of_Communism_in_Poland_(1989) "End of Communism in Poland (1989)"). In Brazil, the majority of Polish immigrants settled in [Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_(state) "Paraná (state)") State. Smaller, but significant numbers settled in the states of [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul"), Espírito Santo and [São Paulo (state)](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"). The city of [Curitiba](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba") has the second largest Polish diaspora in the world (after Chicago) and [Polish music](/wiki/Polish_music "Polish music"), [dishes](/wiki/Polish_cuisine "Polish cuisine") and [culture](/wiki/Polish_culture "Polish culture") are quite common in the region.

[A recent large migration](/wiki/21st_century_economic_migration_of_Poles "21st century economic migration of Poles") of Poles took place following Poland's accession to the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") in 2004 and with the opening of the EU's labor market; an approximate number of 2 million, primarily young, Poles taking up jobs abroad.[[157]](#cite_note-onet-159) It is estimated that over half a million Polish people went to work in the United Kingdom from Poland. Since 2011, Poles have been able to [work freely](/wiki/Freedom_of_movement_for_workers "Freedom of movement for workers") throughout the EU where they have had full working rights since Poland's [EU accession in 2004](/wiki/2004_enlargement_of_the_European_Union "2004 enlargement of the European Union"). The Polish community in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") has increased substantially and has grown to a total number of 120,000, making Poles the largest immigrant group in Norway. Only in recent years has the population abroad decreased, specifically in the UK with 116.000 leaving the UK in 2018 alone. There is a large minority of [Polish people in Ireland](/wiki/Polish_minority_in_Ireland "Polish minority in Ireland") that makes up approximately 2.57% of the population.[[158]](#cite_note-160)
